speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the summons you received the day before the transport was due to leave Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So next morning, around 5 o'clock, or was it 5:30? I'm not sure. Very early in the morning, 30 of us young people were ready to collect the luggage of the remaining close to 1,500 people, 1,400 and odd people. It was 17 [INAUDIBLE]. I-- I should mention one-- one event which sort of interferes with this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we were checked into Bergen-Belsen at the beginning, we had to fill out papers for the Germans telling them where we were born, what we-- our background was, the usual type of papers, and our nationality. And of course most of us put down whatever the truth was, and the nationality was Hungarian. We were citizens of Hungary. There were a few exceptions. Those were the people who were from that part of Hungary which used to belong to Romania and who resented Hungary very much, because Hungary treated them so badly. And they felt they would have been treated much better by Romania, and to give their nationality as Romanian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Turns out that in August 1944, Romania jumped off the Hitler wagon and declared war on Germany. So on December the 2nd, we were notified that we all go to Switzerland except those who declared themselves Romanian citizens, that they would not be liberated, but they would kept in Bergen-Belsen. This involved a number of people, which I don't know the exact number, somewhere between 20 and 40 people. None of them survived Bergen-Belsen. They were all-- they were all-- they all died during the ensuing months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To us it was, you know, quite a trauma to have to say goodbye to these people who really didn't know what they were doing when they simply put down something on a piece of paper saying Romania instead of Hungary. Anyway, next morning we-- we collected the luggage. We went on the trucks to this railroad siding, which was sort of an elliptical elevation. And the trains could come from either side and stop, and then you could just walk into the trains. And we were-- we were kind of curious what kind of trains would-- would these be, because we weren't sure whether we'd go to Auschwitz or whether we'd go to Switzerland. And we were there, the 30 of us. And we said, as soon as we see the train, we'll know. Because if it is a train for people, that is a car-- person cars, passenger cars, then we know we go to Switzerland. If it's cattle cars, like the ones on which we came, then we know we go to Auschwitz. Well, there was nothing there. So we spread the luggage along the lengths of the siding. About one third-- the siding was, say, one unit long, 100 meters long. Around 30 meters from one end, there was a shed. And on the shed there was a sign saying in German that it's forbidden to go beyond this point. There wasn't anybody there, so I went beyond that, and I was caught by an SS man who started beating me with his gun, and I lost consciousness. I woke up in the train. And-- and I never recovered from the back injury that I-- I received there. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now these were regular passenger trains then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular passenger trains, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had ridden in the cattle car type trains from Budapest to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Yeah. Yeah. All those regular deportation trains. We were quite surprised when we saw them, and we had some doubts whether we are really going to Spain and to Palestine. But it was too late to think about it. And there was no light, except through the holes up on-- high on either side. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course there was no toilet and no place to relieve oneself. So it was quite an experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did Dr. Kastner remain among those in Bergen-Belsen until December?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He-- Dr. Kastner did not come with us. He continued his deals with the Germans. He-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, the deal was developing. And other deals were developing. And ultimately, we were let go without practically any payment on the part of-- none-- no 10,000 trucks and no-- no merchandise of any significance was actually delivered. But as time went on, for a variety of reasons-- and this is contested, and Yehuda Bauer, who is one of the leading experts on the Holocaust at Hebrew University has a book on us. Essentially, not only on us, but it's called And it deals to a large extent with the Kastner transport. But as he explains, the-- the real desire of the Germans was to show goodwill after they killed millions and to establish a relationship with-- with the Western powers. But you know, I'm no expert at that. That's not my experience. I shouldn't talk about. Anyway, one can read up on our transport a-- a great deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the journey from Bergen-Belsen to Switzerland like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Took about four days. It was-- it was OK. We couldn't quite make the progress that we were supposed to make because there were constant bombings-- bombings taking place. And by-- and by December 1944, Germany, there wasn't much left of it. And so the train went and then pulled-- pulled back, and tried another way, and finally found its way to the Swiss border. But it was not a-- it was not a straightforward trip. And you know, we stood in various places for a long time, and nothing happened. But we finally arrived in Switzerland. In Lindau, which was the-- which is the Swiss-- the-- the German border town on the Swiss-German border. The-- Dr. Kastner was there. He welcomed us, wished us well. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] German Red Cross nurses gave us food like we never had. And we were treated very well at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel to get out of Germany and be in Switzerland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hard to believe that it was true. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So. And the Swiss gave us incredibly good food. Though we were-- we were really, particularly during October and November, when the new commander was there, we hardly got food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was less than 100 pounds, which was about one half of my normal weight. And so was everybody else. So it was a great relief.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we first stayed for about three days in St. Gallen, which is a town fairly close to the city, 60,000 people at the time. Close to the-- the German border. And then we were taken to the French part of Switzerland, actually, in a resort place for which there were no customers at that time. And we were housed in a resort hotel, which of course was stripped of its luxury features. And there we stayed for several months.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war ended, did you decide to stay in Switzerland, or did you think of returning to Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's a-- that's a very personal thing. The-- obviously we wanted to return to Hungary. Everybody wants to go home. But by the time the war ended, it became pretty clear to most of us that Hungary will be in the Soviet sphere of influence, and that it would be very difficult to continue the kind of life that we were accustomed to in Hungary. So we didn't go back. I went to visit Hungary in 1946 and 1947-- but it was just for a visit-- and returned to Switzerland. And between 1947 and 1975, I wasn't in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- also, after I came to this country in '51, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started working in the electronics industry. And I had the-- I had quite a bit of involvement in the defense industry. So I had a high level security clearance. And that in itself prevented me from visiting Hungary or going beyond the iron curtain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we stayed in the camp. I just discovered yesterday that I left the camp in Switzerland on April the 7th, 1945 and went to the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you just discover that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In looking for papers for you, I-- I run into these papers that I didn't know I had. And there is a-- I found a certificate certifying that I was released on April the 7th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went to Lausanne.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I studied for three days architecture. I-- I knew I had to get off law and political science. I-- I spent three days in the school of architecture and found out that I would never be able to do that. Drafting wasn't my strength. And so I went to the engineering school, studied engineering.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, excuse me. At what point did you learn the extent of what had happened to both the Jews of Hungary and all the Jews of Eastern Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We pretty much knew that before we went to Bergen-Belsen. You see, when-- at the time when we bent to Bergen-Belsen on June the 13th, the country-- not Budapest-- the country was judenreit. Was free of Jews. We were all deported to Auschwitz or elsewhere. The only Jewish community that was left at the time was Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, the only thing that was undecided is who will survive and who will not survive among the people whom we are lost. And, you know, we had good surprises, and we had bad surprises. We had good surprises in that three of the four uncles and aunts whom I mentioned before did survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact that the youngest brother of my father did not survive was, of course, the blow, and a very big blow. He was my-- you know how it is, the youngest brother, the darling of his mother, and so forth. So that was very bad. And then my grandmother in Budapest. My grandmother in Budapest was killed on January the 14th--

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, Four days before the liberation of Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned that after the war. She was killed in the Budapest Orthodox [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hospital, where she was killed together with all the other patients, doctors, nurses, everybody. The-- the details are well known and written up. That was a-- a band of [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] volunteer-- volunteer Nazis, Hungarian Nazis, under the leadership of Father Kun, who was a crazy Catholic priest who-- who, you know, just went in and exterminated everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now the family that went to Switzerland, was everybody able to comfortably acclimate to being in a new country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, with-- we had difficulties. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, that-- yes and no. There were some people who weren't able to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: My girlfriend, Naomi, she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she came with us. I went to see Dr. Kastner at 6 o'clock in the morning on 30 June, 1944, in his office. I tried to get him for two or three days before that. I knew that our transport was leaving, and I could never get to him. He was so busy. And then I reached him by phone the day before. And he said, if you come to my office at 6:00 am tomorrow morning, and I'll see you. I went to his office, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said Dr. Kastner, I would like my girlfriend to come with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Noemi, Naomi in English, Meyer. And he said, what's her name? Took a piece of paper for same form that all of us had, rolled it up, signed it, and said OK [INAUDIBLE]. She committed suicide up on the ride from Switzerland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were affected in very different ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- one of my cousins attempted to commit suicide. You know, it's-- the-- the-- the trauma is-- is very different.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had been your girlfriend in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less. I wanted her to be my girlfriend. But then we were together in the camp. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] most-- most people did survive reasonably well, got rid of their traumas, got rid of their physical ailments. My-- my mother was in very bad-- in very bad health when we arrived. But she-- well, she lived for many years thereafter. She recovered. Spent several weeks in the hospital in St. Gallen. Didn't come with us to the camp to be supported. My mother was in difficult shape.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you completed your degree, did you remain in Switzerland then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. I went to work for a Swiss firm first, for about a year. And then I went to teach at the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology for about a year and a half. Then I came to the US on this odd trip. We-- we applied for-- my father applied for all of us for an American immigration visa as soon as we got to Switzerland, early 1945. And we were notified in late 1950 that the visas have arrived. Because the Hungarian quota was very bad at the time. And so I didn't want to go because I felt I had a good job in Switzerland and was on my way to becoming a professor. My dad said he'll pay for the trip, and so I went and liked it, and then went back to Switzerland for half a year to complete my contract with the Institute. And then in August '51 I came definitively to the US, joined General Electric.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only member of your family who ultimately settled in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, quite a few members of our family came to the States. My parents did not. They were more the exception. Several of my uncles came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents remained in Switzerland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did they live after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad died in '85, and my mother died in '86. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they came us-- they came-- they visited us regularly, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until they became advanced in age. But you know, for many years thereafter, we-- I didn't talk about the Holocaust to anybody. I became deeply involved in my profession. I've had fairly exciting experiences professionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just was something I tried to suppress in my mind, up to a certain point when it came up. And I'm not trying to suppress it today at all. But you try to be normal. One tried to be normal. One tried not to, you know, call attention to oneself with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] odd stories that most people wouldn't understand anyway, that would fill them with horror, and-- and that maybe people wouldn't believe because they would say that such a thing isn't possible. You know, I remember such a thing isn't possible. Very early after the occupation of Hungary, it must have been either in late March or in early April, I stepped out of the Sheep Street 12. That was the headquarters of the Jewish council where I worked. And I ran into a friend of mine, Paul Bergman. Paul Bergman was one of the 48. He was a student of mathematics, second year. He was senior to me by a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we chatted with each other on the street, just opposite the entrance. I see it as if it were today. And I said to Paul, what are you going to do? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, I have nothing to fear. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he takes out a certificate. His father was the owner of what was briefly called the large silver. That was the large silver decoration of World War I, earned for bravery in action. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked at him and I said, what are you going to do with this paper? And he said, I'm just going to show it, like I'm showing it to you. That will exempt me from any unpleasantness. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said Paul, you're out of your mind. You don't know what's going on. And he says, Arthur, this is the 20th century. These stories that you are telling me are not true. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been in, say, mid-April, 1944. Paul Bergman was deported and died. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he was-- he was characteristic of many, many Hungarian Jews who felt it cannot happen. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before you left Switzerland and moved to the United States for good, did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I was very reluctant to get married. I met my wife in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith Samuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September 1952. It was a very complicated story. First we couldn't get married because my parents objected. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was a Western Jew and non-Orthodox, and we were Eastern Jews and Orthodox, so that's like marrying out of the faith. And then, frankly, well, I had-- I had cold feet up to the last moment. It's such a big responsibility.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then finally, I did it. I came back from-- from the US, and we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you settled in New York, how many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we settled in Syracuse, New York, where I was with General Electric. And we had three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daniel, '54, Claude, '55--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the year that they were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born, yeah. And Jackie, '58. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and we were very lucky. They are good kids. They were good of kids to have. And they are very successful, each of them in their own ways. And the only thing that's wrong is that they are away from here. So we have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have-- do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have several grandchildren. Danny has two girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- what are their-- Danny has two girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danny has two girls, Joanna and Julia. They are 11 and seven. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Claude has two boys. They are two and a half and zero.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob and Joshua.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say zero, he was born recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born like three weeks ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: In January of '96?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And Jackie has three, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ilana, who is the oldest one. She must be around five, six. Six. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, six. And Gabriel, who is four, going on five. And Nathan, who will be one, I believe, in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I--

speaker: interviewee
text: So things turned out much better than I expected at some times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know you-- you have a photograph you're going to show of them. But before we look at your photographs, is there anything you would like to add about your experiences in the years prior to and during the war that you feel you left out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I feel I did not really do justice to-- to the role played by my father, which I was able to observe, in rescuing Jews during the years immediately preceding our Holocaust. That is, immediately preceding March 19. He was-- he was a very unusual person, and I-- I feel privileged that I was able to observe him during these years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a very ambitious person. Ambitious for position, for influence, for hobnobbing with the Hungarian aristocracy whose-- whose forests he bought for his lumber mills. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a learned man, in a Jewish sense. He had a yeshiva background. And he-- he was modern Orthodox. By that I mean that he was quite Orthodox, but he, you know, didn't wear a beard, and he was a man of the world. And starting about 1941, there was this tremendous change in this man, where he gradually abandoned all his business involvements, leaving it essentially up to his brother, and where he concentrated his life completely on the rescue mission. And-- which then really culminated in-- in the occupation, when he refused to join the Jewish council. He was told by General Muzovski-- he was sort of an-- an-- an interesting influence. The general convinced him that if he joins the Jewish Council-- not as employee, like I did, as a little guy, but as a-- in a capacity as a member of the council, a policy-making member of the council, that he would become a traitor to his people. That-- that it was unavoidable. That all Polish Jewish council members finally had to wind up in that capacity. And he refused to cooperate. He-- he became very much convinced of that and withdrew. He accepted the protection papers, but he did not do anything but charitable activities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the fact that he-- that he weathered the-- the threatened ban by the [NON-ENGLISH], and that he actually, you know, had the guts to say, this is a situation where we have to cooperate with the godless Zionists, in my mind makes him a-- well, somewhat of a historic figure. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I guess I didn't talk much about him during the-- during the interview. And I-- I somehow feel I should have done more justice to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think you just did. And is there any final message you'd like to leave with the people who will be seeing this tape in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess there is one thing that-- which is a very personal thing to me. The Jewish people today is somewhat divided about issues of peace and not peace. But I've mentioned a few times to my friends that when in Bergen-Belsen I was unable to sleep at night--

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what, I'm sorry to interrupt, but we--
